nativeRange.habitat: The Broadstripe Goby inhabits coral reefs in the western Atlantic and Caribbean, found at depths from the surface down to around 25 m, where adults tend to associate with coral heads.

diet.notes: In the wild, has been documented functioning as a cleaner fish, picking ectoparasites from host fish, but also consuming coral polyps, sponge tissue and spicules — indicating it does not rely exclusively on cleaning. In captivity, has been known to accept frozen mysis shrimp, live and frozen brine shrimp, and other small meaty foods typical for Elacatinus gobies.

summary: A tiny Caribbean coral-dwelling goby reaching about 4 cm, the Broadstripe Goby is generally considered peaceful and reef-safe. Tends to associate with coral heads and has been documented functioning as a cleaner fish.
careNotes:
The Broadstripe Goby is generally advised to be kept in a well-established reef aquarium with ample live rock and coral heads, which it will use as shelter and territory. A tight-fitting lid is commonly recommended, as Elacatinus gobies are known to jump or squeeze through small openings.

Although documented as a cleaner fish in the wild, gut-content studies on E. prochilos have found coral polyps, sponge tissues, and sponge spicules alongside parasites from hosts, indicating it does not rely exclusively on cleaning. In captivity, small meaty frozen foods — such as frozen mysis shrimp and brine shrimp — are commonly accepted.

The Broadstripe Goby is listed as Vulnerable (VU) on the IUCN Red List; wild-caught specimens may be less frequently available than captive-bred relatives, and preference for captive-bred individuals is advised where they can be sourced. In the wild, adults are observed forming groups near coral heads, and the species is documented as monogamous. Keeping a mated pair in the aquarium is commonly suggested for gobies in this genus.
breedingNotes: Like other gobies in the genus Elacatinus, the Broadstripe Goby is believed to deposit adhesive eggs on hard surfaces such as crevice walls or shell interiors. Captive spawning has been documented in related species within the same genus, suggesting this species may similarly spawn under appropriate conditions. Detailed captive-breeding reports specific to E. prochilos remain limited.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: LiveAquaria's goby collection page did not list Elacatinus prochilos directly; no individual species product page was found. Husbandry parameters for the closely related E. oceanops (Neon Blue Goby, same genus) were cross-referenced from LiveAquaria product page as a primary analog.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Primary species-specific source. Confirmed: max size 4.0 cm TL; family Gobiidae, order Gobiiformes; range Western Central Atlantic (southern Florida and Lesser Antilles); habitat reef-associated 0–25 m depth; temperature 26.7–28.3°C; trophic level 3.15 (carnivore/cleaner); monogamous; adults in groups near coral heads. IUCN VU status confirmed. Temperature disagrees with LiveAquaria analog data for E. oceanops (22.2–25.6°C); averaged to 24.0–27.5°C.

additional.name: FishBase Ecology
additional.notes: Confirmed diet details: does not rely exclusively on cleaning; gut contents include coral polyps, sponge tissue and spicules in addition to ectoparasites from hosts.

additional.name: LiveAquaria (Neon Blue Goby analog)
additional.notes: Cross-reference for Elacatinus oceanops (same genus, similar ecology). Provided: temperature 72–78°F (22.2–25.6°C); salinity 1.020–1.025; pH 8.1–8.4; dKH 8–12; diet carnivore; minimum tank 10 gal (38 L); care level easy; reef safe yes; peaceful temperament. Applied to E. prochilos with temperature averaged against FishBase wild data.

additional.name: fws.gov
additional.notes: Secondary source listed in manifest. FWS species profile confirmed scientific name (Elacatinus prochilos, TSN 636788) but contained no populated husbandry or biological content beyond the name confirmation.

fish.breedingNotes: Has been reared in captivity. Like related Elacatinus species, likely deposits adhesive eggs in crevices; males probably guard eggs. Captive spawning reports for this specific species are limited.
